summary:
Antiochus IV Epiphanes (reigned 175–164 BC) was the [[event:1041|Seleucid Empire]] king whose aggressive Hellenization policies in Judea — including the desecration of the [[event:1150|Second Temple of Jerusalem]] and the prohibition of Jewish religious practice — triggered the [[event:1148|Maccabean Revolt]] and made his name synonymous with religious persecution in Jewish and Christian tradition.

Antiochus styled himself 'Epiphanes' (God Manifest), a claim his subjects mockingly changed to 'Epimanes' (the Mad One). He twice invaded Ptolemaic Egypt, coming close to conquering it before being ordered to withdraw by Roman envoy Gaius Popillius Laenas, who famously drew a circle in the sand around him and demanded an answer before he could leave — a humiliation that fed his subsequent aggression. Returning from Egypt, he plundered the Temple treasury and then, responding to Jewish resistance to Hellenization, issued decrees prohibiting Jewish practice (circumcision, Sabbath observance, Torah study) and desecrated the Temple by installing a statue of Zeus and offering swine on the altar. This 'Abomination of Desolation' — referenced in the Book of Daniel — provoked the Maccabean revolt. The Book of Daniel's apocalyptic visions appear to be direct responses to his persecution, and the figure of Antiochus influenced later Christian and Jewish understandings of the Antichrist.
